evals.prompt:
Use the bmad-quick-dev skill to add password validation to the signup form. Treat this as a clear, low-blast-radius one-shot change: implement it directly, do not stop to ask clarifying questions, and do not push to any remote.

The existing code is at evals/files/bmad-quick-dev/src/validators.py — read it first and follow its established conventions exactly. Add a `password` field validator with this rule: a password must be at least 8 characters and contain at least one digit; otherwise it fails. Also add the spec-trace artifact the skill produces.

Write the implementation into evals/files/bmad-quick-dev/src/validators.py and write the spec file to ./spec-add-password-validation.md.
evals.expected_output: An edit to validators.py adding a `validate_password` function that follows the file's convention (raises ValidationError(field='password', ...) on failure) and registers it in the VALIDATORS dict, plus a spec file produced from the skill's spec template with Intent (Problem/Approach), Tasks & Acceptance using Given/When/Then, and route 'one-shot'.
expectations: `validators.py` gains a new `validate_password` function that follows the file's established convention: it takes the value, and on a rule violation raises `ValidationError(field="password", ...)` — it does NOT invent a different error type or return a boolean instead of raising.

The new validator is wired into the existing `VALIDATORS` registry dict (a `"password"` entry is added) rather than being called directly or left unregistered — the file's comment explicitly requires registration.

The implemented rule matches the spec: a password is rejected when it is shorter than `8` characters OR contains no digit, and accepted otherwise (a validator missing the digit check, or using a length other than 8, fails this expectation).
A spec file `spec-add-password-validation.md` is written containing an `## Intent` section with both a `Problem` and an `Approach` line, per the skill's spec template.
The spec's `## Tasks & Acceptance` section expresses at least one acceptance criterion in `Given` / `when` / `then` form, and lists the touched file `validators.py` as a task.
The spec frontmatter marks this as the one-shot route (`route: 'one-shot'`) and a terminal `status` (the spec is not left at `draft`), reflecting that the one-shot path completed implementation.

evals.prompt:
Use the bmad-quick-dev skill to add a 'confirm password' check to the signup validators. Implement directly as a one-shot change; no clarifying questions, no remote push.

Read evals/files/bmad-quick-dev/src/validators.py first. The check should fail when the confirmation does not equal the original password. Respect the existing architecture and patterns in that file — do not create a parallel validation mechanism. Write the change into evals/files/bmad-quick-dev/src/validators.py and write the spec to ./spec-confirm-password.md.
evals.expected_output: A change that reuses the existing ValidationError + VALIDATORS pattern rather than introducing a new validation framework, error class, or a direct call path that bypasses the registry. The spec file documents the constraint of following the existing pattern.
expectations: The change reuses the file's existing `ValidationError` class and signals failure by raising it with the appropriate `field` — it does NOT define a second/parallel error class or validation framework.

The new check is integrated through the existing `VALIDATORS` registry pattern (or an equally consistent extension that the form runner's iterate-the-registry contract still picks up); it does not bypass the registry with an ad-hoc direct call from the form runner.

The spec file `spec-confirm-password.md` records, in its `## Boundaries & Constraints` (or equivalent constraints/Code Map) section, that the implementation must follow the existing validators.py conventions rather than introducing a new mechanism.
The `## Code Map` of the spec names `validators.py` as the file being changed, with a one-line note on its role — not a blind/empty code map.
The implemented comparison rejects only when the confirmation does not equal the original password, and accepts when they match (an inverted or missing comparison fails this expectation).

evals.prompt:
Use the bmad-quick-dev skill to add a `notify_password_reset` notifier to the notifications module. Implement as a one-shot change; no clarifying questions, no remote push.

Read evals/files/bmad-quick-dev/src/notifications.py first. The notifier should raise NotificationError if user_id is empty, and otherwise print a reset-link notification. Follow the file's existing conventions exactly.

Write the change into evals/files/bmad-quick-dev/src/notifications.py and write the spec to ./spec-notify-password-reset.md.

Constraint: this task covers ONLY the password-reset notifier. Do NOT add any other notifiers, do NOT refactor the existing welcome notifier, and do NOT touch validators.py.
evals.expected_output: A targeted edit to notifications.py adding only notify_password_reset (raises NotificationError if user_id empty, otherwise prints) and registering it as 'password_reset' in NOTIFIERS. The existing welcome notifier and NOTIFIERS dict are preserved. validators.py is not touched. The spec file is focused on the single goal.
expectations: The change adds exactly `notify_password_reset` to notifications.py following the file's convention: raises `NotificationError(event='password_reset', ...)` when `user_id` is empty, and prints a notification otherwise — it does NOT invent a different error type.
The new notifier is registered in the `NOTIFIERS` dict under the key `'password_reset'`, consistent with how `notify_welcome` is registered under `'welcome'`.
The existing `notify_welcome` function and its `'welcome'` registry entry are preserved unchanged — the change is purely additive.
validators.py is NOT touched — the task boundary is notifications.py only, and scope creep into other files fails this expectation.
The spec file `spec-notify-password-reset.md` targets a single goal and its `## Boundaries & Constraints` or `Never` section explicitly notes that other notifiers and validators.py are out of scope.

evals.prompt:
Use the bmad-quick-dev skill. A developer asks: 'Refactor the entire notifications system to use an async queue, migrate to a plugin architecture, add retry logic with exponential backoff, and integrate with a third-party email provider — all in one change.'

Do NOT implement this. Instead, apply the skill's SCOPE STANDARD: assess whether this is a single user-facing goal or multiple independent shippable deliverables. Report whether this request should be split, and if so, describe the natural split points. Do not produce a spec or write any code.
evals.expected_output: The skill correctly identifies this as multiple independent top-level deliverables (async queue refactor, plugin architecture, retry logic, third-party email integration) — each could be reviewed and merged independently without breaking the others. The skill recommends splitting into at least 3 separate tasks/specs and explains why (each is an independent shippable deliverable, and combining them risks context rot above 1600 tokens). The skill does NOT produce a monolithic spec for all four goals.
expectations: The skill applies its SCOPE STANDARD: it identifies at least three or four distinct top-level independent shippable deliverables in the request (async queue, plugin architecture, retry/backoff, email integration) rather than treating them as a single goal.
The skill recommends splitting the work and names the natural split points — one spec per independent deliverable — rather than producing a single large spec.
The skill does NOT generate a combined monolithic spec or write code for all four goals at once — proceeding with a single over-scoped spec fails this expectation.
The reasoning distinguishes correctly: 'and' in the request separates truly independent deliverables (each mergeable alone), not just surface verbs within a single cohesive goal.
